topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The key felt cold and heavy in my palm, a leaden weight mirroring the sudden drop in my stomach. Grandma’s house loomed before us, all peeling paint and shadowed eaves, a monument to things I didn't quite understand. My brother, Mark, was already halfway up the porch steps, his laughter bright and careless, a stark contrast to the tightness gripping my chest. I stood rooted to the spot, my gaze snagging on a faded photograph tucked into the mantelpiece—a picture of a woman with eyes that seemed to hold a quiet sorrow. Every sound outside, the rustle of leaves, the distant drone of traffic, seemed amplified, pressing in on my ears until I wanted to shrink into the wallpaper. I just wanted to disappear into the dust motes dancing in the weak afternoon light.

[story 2]

"It’s just an old house, Maya. It’s full of history, not ghosts," Mark said, clapping me on the shoulder with a force that made me flinch. I managed a small, tight smile that didn't reach my eyes. The air in the entryway smelled overwhelmingly of lavender and decay, a cloying combination that made my throat feel dry. I moved slowly, tracing the intricate pattern of the worn wooden floorboards with the toe of my slipper. Each step was deliberate, as if navigating a minefield. I found myself clinging to the railing of the staircase, my knuckles white against the cool wood, watching the shadows lengthen and distort the familiar shapes of the rooms. A tremor started deep in my hands, and I quickly tucked them into my pockets, focusing intently on the pattern of the rug, anything to keep the rising tide of unease from spilling over.

[story 3]

I stood by the window, the glass cool against my forehead. The sheer volume of memories packed into this place was staggering, overwhelming the space between my thoughts. When Mark started planning how we’d divide the furniture, his cheerful voice sounded like grating sandpaper. I didn't look at him. Instead, I focused on the intricate carving on the window frame, my breath catching in shallow, quick bursts. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, a hot, prickly sensation that made my vision swim slightly. I needed quiet. I needed distance. I backed away from the main living area, retreating toward the narrow hallway, seeking the small, dim space where the silence felt less like an accusation and more like a fragile shield against the world.
